findings.implication: Scientific Abstract: Clearly states the problem (health inequalities in IBD), objectives, methods (mixed-methods, 3 phases), beneficiaries (SA populations), and impact (policy, equity), supporting pr.1.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines a feasible 3-phase study with specific data sources (CPRD, HES), sample sizes (30-40 interviews), and timelines (2005-2025), supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Details rigorous methods including Cox regression, Poisson models, interpretive phenomenology, and RAMESES framework for realist review, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly aims to improve outcomes for SA patients, inform policy, and align with NHS Long Term Plan and CORE20PLUS5, supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: Plans recruitment via patient charities (CCUK, SAIA) and includes stakeholder engagement in Phase 3, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Focuses specifically on South Asian communities to address ethnic disparities and cultural barriers, supporting pr.9.
Scientific Abstract: Includes interviews with patients to explore lived experiences and stakeholder engagement to refine theories, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Aims to produce actionable recommendations and transferable strategies to enhance equity and improve patient outcomes, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Targets South Asian populations, a specific ethnic minority group often underserved in IBD care, supporting wp.4.
Scientific Abstract: Integrates diverse perspectives (quantitative data, qualitative lived experience, evidence synthesis) across all phases to address equity, supporting wp.5.
